content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 11, 2000


James Cathcart
1543 Main Street
Napa, California 94559

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-00-175

Dear Mr. Cathcart:

This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance regarding the conflict of interest provisions¹ of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").²

QUESTIONS

Decision 1: May you, as a member of the 25ᵗʰ District Agricultural Association, participate in a decision to approve a specific development that is similar to, and six miles from, a proposed project in which your employer has invested?

Decision 2: May you vote on any general policy direction with respect to development of the fairgrounds for a hotel or conference center?

Decision 3: May you vote on whether development of the fairgrounds should be controlled by the District or by another entity? —

Decision 4: May you vote on whether or not the Association should have its own private attorney to represent the Association for its developmental issues?


¹ Please note that the Commission has adopted amendments to the conflict of interest regulations discussed below. Where applicable, we have indicated whether new standards will apply.

² Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18896, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. I-00-175
Page 2

CONCLUSION

For each decision, you may not participate if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will result in a material financial effect on one of your economic interests. You must make a
good faith effort to assess the effect of the decision by using some reasonable and objective
method of valuation.

FACTS

Based on your correspondence and phone conversations with Commission staff counsel,
you are a member of the 25th District Agricultural Association ("Association"). This
Association, established by California Food and Agricultural Code Section 3877, is responsible
for holding fairs and constructing, maintaining, and operating fairground facilities.

You are currently employed by the Doctor's Company ("company") as Vice President of
Governmental Relations and are compensated in two ways. You receive a monthly salary and an
annual bonus. The bonus amount is dependent upon how well the company does as a whole and
how well you perform your duties according to a set of predetermined Key Performance
Indicators.

The company's primary business is selling medical malpractice insurance to physicians.
It collects premiums and invests those premiums in a variety of investments as determined by the
California Department of Insurance. In the past, the company has developed a hotel property in
the City of Yountville. Currently, your employer has an application with Napa County for a 300
bed hotel and business conference center to be located adjacent to its headquarters building on
the corner of Highways 12 and 29. The proposed project of the company is approximately six
miles south of any proposed development of a similar nature that might occur at the 25th
Agricultural District Association fairgrounds. While the resulting development may accord the
company with a fair return on its investment, the investment will be small in relation to its total
investment portfolio (invested assets and reserves are approximately one billion dollars). The
company had a pre-tax income for the last fiscal year of at least $2,500,000. The company is not
listed on the exchanges or lists identified in Regulation 18705.1 of California Code of
Regulations, Title 2, Division 6 which interprets provisions of the Act.

To the extent that the company's development occurs and is successful, it could reflect on
the overall profit of the company (however small) and, hence, translate into an increased bonus
amount to be divided among its employees.

As Vice President of Governmental Relations, you are primarily responsible for
developing political strategies to address liability issues in state and federal jurisdictions. You do
not perform any such activities for the company in relation to its investment projects nor are any

File No. I-00-175
Page 3

such activities reflected in your individual Key Performance Indicators, or in your job
classification criteria.

You have indicated that your employer has not initiated a proceeding before the
Association, is not a named party or subject of a proceeding before the Association, and does not
plan to engage in these actions.

ANALYSIS

The primary purpose for the conflict of interest provisions of the Act is to ensure that
public officials, whether elected or appointed, perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have
supported them. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.

Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under Section 87100 of the Act requires
analysis of the following questions³:

Step One: Are you a "public official" within the meaning of the rules?

As a member of the 25ᵗʰ District Agricultural Association, established by California Food
and Agricultural Code Section 3877, you are a "member, officer, employee, or consultant of a
state or local government agency" and, therefore, are subject to the conflict of interest provisions
of the Act. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a).)

Step Two: Will you make, participate in making, or influence a governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in making a
governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and without
significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes
recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87100;
Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence a decision before his or her own agency if, for the purpose of influencing, the official

³ These questions are based on the Act's conflict of interest analysis provided at Regulation 18700(b). The
Commission document "Can I Vote? Conflicts of Interest Overview" explains the steps of this analysis and is
enclosed for your information.

File No. I-00-175
Page 4

contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18702.3.)

As a member of the 25th District Agricultural Association, you will "make a
governmental decision" if you vote on a specific fairground development, vote on general policy
direction regarding development of the fairgrounds, vote on whether development of the
fairgrounds should be controlled by the District, or vote on whether the Association should have
its own private attorney represent the Association on its development issues. Additionally, you
will "participate in making" or "influence" a governmental decision if you perform any of the
actions detailed above with respect to these decisions.

Step Three: What are your economic interests? — The possible sources of a financial
conflict of interest.

You have inquired and provided facts regarding only your employer. For purposes of this
letter, we assume that you have no other economic interests relevant to the governmental
decisions about which you inquire. Therefore, we analyze only the economic interests derived
from your company.

As such, you have two economic interests which may potentially create a conflict of
interest under the Act:

1. Source of Income

A public official has an economic interest in any person from whom he or she has
received income aggregating $250⁴ within 12 months prior to the time when the relevant
governmental decision is made. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3(a).) Assuming salary
and bonus payments to you from your employer aggregate to $250 within 12 months prior to the
time when one of the Association's decisions is made, you have an economic interest in your
employer.

2. Personal Financial Effect

A governmental decision has a personal financial effect on a public official if the decision
will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) Therefore, if
any of the prospective decisions by the Association will result in an increase or decrease your
income, you will experience a personal financial effect as a result of that decision.

⁴ Because Section 87103 has been amended as of January 1, 2001, this amount will increase to $500.

File No. I-00-175
Page 5

Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in each
governmental decision?

1. Source of Income

A person, including a source of income, is directly involved in a decision before an
official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person." (Regulation 18704.1(a).)

In the facts you have provided, you indicated that your employer has not initiated a
proceeding before the Association, is not a named party or subject of a proceeding before the
Association, and does not plan to engage in these actions. As a result, your employer is not
directly involved in any of the prospective decisions you have identified.

2. Personal Financial Effect

A public official, or his or her immediate family, are deemed to be directly involved in a
governmental decision which has any personal financial effect on him or her, or his or her
immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation 18704.5(a).) Consequently, if any of the
decisions of the Association will have a personal financial effect on you, then you will be
directly involved in this governmental decision. This rule also applies to any other decision of
the Association.

Step Five: Materiality standard — which financial impacts on your economic interests
from the decision are considered material?

1. Source of Income

Where a source of income which is a business entity is not directly involved in a
governmental decision, the materiality standards provided by Regulation 18705.1(b) apply,
unless the effect of a decision is deemed material under the "nexus test" (discussed below).
(Regulations 18704.1(b) and 18705.3(b)(1); Bennett Advice Letter, No. A-98-239.)

File No. I-00-175
Page 6

Under Regulation 18705.1, the effect of a decision is material for any business entity which has
net tangible assets of at least $18,000,000 and which had pre-tax income for the last fiscal year of
at least $2,500,000 if:

(A) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
gross revenues for a fiscal year of $150,000 or more; or
(B) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $50,000 or more; or
(C) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of assets or liabilities of $150,000 or more.
(Regulation 18705.1(b)(2).)⁵

Because your employer has assets of one billion dollars and had a pre-tax income for the
last fiscal year of at least $2,500,000, the materiality standard applies to any decision of the
Association in which your employer is indirectly involved, unless the nexus test is met. The
nexus test prohibits a public official from accomplishing, as an official, that for which the official
receives compensation in his or her private capacity. (Bennett Advice Letter, No. A-98-239.)
Under the nexus test, any reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision is deemed to be material if
there is a nexus between the purpose for which the official received income and the governmental
decision. (Ibid.) You have indicated that your job duties do not include any responsibilities
related to the investment projects of the company. Therefore, the nexus test is not met, and the
materiality standard of Regulation 18705.1(b)(2), provided above, is applicable.⁶

2. Personal Financial Effect

A reasonably foreseeable personal financial effect is material if it is at least $250 in any
12 month period. (Section 87100; Regulation 18705.5.) Consequently, if it is reasonably
foreseeable that you will receive $250 or more within a year from your employer as a result of
any of the decisions, then you will have a conflict of interest.

___________________________

⁵ This materiality standard applies to business entities not covered by 18705.1(b)(1) and (2). (Regulation
18705.1(b)(5).)

⁶ At its December 8, 2000 meeting, the Commission approved draft regulations which revise the
materiality standards for business entities. We anticipate these amendments to be effective in the first part of next
year. Please check our website at the time of your decision for further information on the date these revised
standards will be implemented.

File No. I-00-175
Page 7

Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will result in the
materiality standard for each economic interest being met?

A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it is
substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards applicable to that economic
interest will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Section 87100; Regulation 18706.)

1. Source of Income.

Decision 1: To determine if the effect of Decision 1 will affect your employer in such a way
that the monetary thresholds of Regulation 18705.1(b)(2)(A) are met, we must analyze the facts
regarding your employer's investment and the possible effects of a similar development on your
employer's gross revenues, expenses, assets, or liabilities. With regard to the materiality
standards of 18705.1, Commission staff has previously advised that a public official's estimation
of the probable financial impact of such a decision must be a reasonable one. (O'Harra Advice
Letter, No. A-00-174.) You must make a good faith effort to assess the effect of the decision by
using some reasonable and objective method of valuation. A public official is not required to,
but may choose to obtain a professional appraisal, such as a real estate appraisal, to assist in
assessing the financial effect of a decision for conflict of interest purposes.

Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when issuing advice, we cannot
advise you regarding whether your employer will experience a material financial effect as a result
of the decision. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Ultimately, as the public official with
the potential conflict, you bear the responsibility of deciding whether a material financial effect
is reasonably foreseeable. Please be aware that the Commission is cognizant of the complexities
faced by a public official in assessing foreseeability and plans to address this issue as part of its
2001 Project C examination into possible standards of care to be applied to those subject to the
Act. If you are interested in this or other projects of the Commission, you may wish to view
information posted on our website: www.fppc.ca.gov.

Decisions 2 and 3: Generally, decisions are analyzed independently to determine if there will
be a foreseeable material financial effect on an official's financial interest. (In re Owen, 2 FPPC
Ops. 77 (1976). However, certain decisions are too interrelated to be considered separately, and
in that event, a public official's conflict on one decision will be disqualifying for the other. (Ball
Advice Letter, No. A-98-124.) Specifically, a public official would have to disqualify himself or
herself if the result of one decision would effectively determine or nullify the result of another.
(Ibid.) For example, in a decision to select one of two autopark sites, the determination to select
one of the sites is essentially a decision against the other autopark site (Boogaard Advice Letter,
No. I-90-347.) Similarly, Decisions 2 and 3 appear to be interrelated to the decision whether or
not to approve a specific development similar to that of your employer since Decisions 2 and 3
must necessarily precede Decision 1 in order for it to be made. Therefore, if you determine that
you have a conflict with respect to Decision 1, then you will be prohibited from participating in

File No. I-00-175
Page 8

Decisions 2 and 3. Conversely, if you conclude that you do not have a conflict with respect to
Decision 1, you may participate in Decisions 2 and 3.

Decision 4: You have not provided the facts necessary for us to analyze this question.
Specifically, we require additional information to determine whether a material financial effect
on one of your economic interests will result from this decision. As such, if you have other
economic interests which are directly or indirectly involved in the decision of the Association to
hire its own private attorney to work on its development issues, you must provide us with this
information in order for us to determine whether you will have a conflict of interest in this
decision. If you have no other relevant economic interests, you must assess whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the hiring of an attorney by the Association would result in a material
financial effect on your employer.

2. Personal Financial Effect.

Decisions 1-4: If it is reasonably foreseeable that an increase or decrease of at least $250
in your annual bonus results from any of the decisions you identified, you will have a
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Personal Financial Effect rule. Based on your facts, in
making this determination, you must refer to your assessment of the financial effect of each
decision on your employer. Then, you must determine how much your bonus will increase or
decrease as a result of each decision. Since additional intervening factors such as the Key
Performance Indicators will act to affect the amount of money you will receive in your annual
bonus, it is clear that the foreseeability of meeting the Personal Financial Effect materiality
standard will be less than the foreseeability of a material financial effect on the company itself.
You should consider this conclusion in evaluating whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
materiality standard for your personal finances will be met.

Steps Seven and Eight are discussed in the enclosed Conflict of Interest Overview. If you
have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: [signature]
Natalie Bocanegra
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

be a foreseeable material financial effect on an official's financial interest. (In re Owen,2 FPPC
Ops. 77 (1976). However, certain decisions are too interrelated to be considered separately, and
in that event, a public official's conflict on one decision will be disqualiffing for the other (Ball
Advice Letter, No. A-98-124.) Specifically, a public official would have to disqualiff himself or
herself if the result of one decision would effectively determine or nulliff the result of another.
(Ibid.) For example, in a decision to select one of two autopark sites, the determination to select
one of the sites is essentially a decision against the other autopark site (Boogaard Advice Letter,
No. I-90-347.) Similarly, Decisions 2 and 3 appear to be interrelated to the decision whether or
not to approve a specific development similar to that of your employer since Decisions 2 and 3
must necessarily precede Decision 1 in order for it to be made. Therefore, if you determine that
you have a conflict with respect to Decision 1, then you will be prohibited from participating in
Decisions 2 and 3. Conversely, if you conclude that you do not have a conflict with respect to
Decision 1, you may participate in Decisions 2 and3.
Decision 4: You have not provided the facts necessary for us to analyze this question.
Specifically, we require additional information to determine whether a material financial effect
on one of your economic interests will result from this decision. As such, if you have other
economic interests which are directly or indirectly involved in the decision of the Association to
hire its own private attorney to work on its development issuqs, you must provide us with this
information in order for us to determine whether you will have a conflict of interest in this
decision. If you have no other relevant economic interests, you must assess whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the hiring of an attomey by the Association would result in a material
financial effect on your employer.
2. Personal Financial Effect.
Decisions 1- 4: If it is reasonably foreseeable that an increase or decrease of at least $250
in your annual bonus results from any of the decisions you identified, you will have a
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Personal Financial Effect rule. Based on your facts, in
making this determination, you must refer to your assessment of the financial effect of each
decision on your employer. Then, you must determine how much your bonus will increase or
decrease as a result of each decision. Since additional intervening factors such as the Key
Performance Indicators will act to affect the amount of money you will receive in your annual
bonus, it is clear that the foreseeability of meeting the Personal Financial Effect materiality
standard will be less than the foreseeability of a material financial effect on the company itself.
You should consider this conclusion in evaluating whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
materiality standard for your personal finances will be met.
Steps Seven and Eight are discussed in the enclosed Conflict of Interest Overview. If you
have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

QUESTION: Decision 1: May you, as a member of the 25n District Agricultural Association, participate in a
decision to approve a specific development that is similar to, and six miles from, a proposed
project in which your employer has invested?
Decision 2: May you vote on any general policy direction with respect to development of the
fairgrounds for a hotel or conference center?
Decision 3: May you vote on whether development of the fairgrounds should be controlled by
the District or by another entity?
Decision 4: May you vote on whether or not the Association should have its own private
attorney to represent the Association for its developmental issues?

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 December 11, 2000 James Cathcart 1543 Main Street Napa, California 94559 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-00-175 Dear Mr. Cathcart: This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance regarding the conflict of interest provisions¹ of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").² QUESTIONS Decision 1: May you, as a member of the 25ᵗʰ District Agricultural Association, participate in a decision to approve a specific development that is similar to, and six miles from, a proposed project in which your employer has invested? Decision 2: May you vote on any general policy direction with respect to development of the fairgrounds for a hotel or conference center? Decision 3: May you vote on whether development of the fairgrounds should be controlled by the District or by another entity? — Decision 4: May you vote on whether or not the Association should have its own private attorney to represent the Association for its developmental issues? ¹ Please note that the Commission has adopted amendments to the conflict of interest regulations discussed below. Where applicable, we have indicated whether new standards will apply. ² Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18896, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. I-00-175 Page 2 CONCLUSION For each decision, you may not participate if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will result in a material financial effect on one of your economic interests. You must make a good faith effort to assess the effect of the decision by using some reasonable and objective method of valuation. FACTS Based on your correspondence and phone conversations with Commission staff counsel, you are a member of the 25th District Agricultural Association ("Association"). This Association, established by California Food and Agricultural Code Section 3877, is responsible for holding fairs and constructing, maintaining, and operating fairground facilities. You are currently employed by the Doctor's Company ("company") as Vice President of Governmental Relations and are compensated in two ways. You receive a monthly salary and an annual bonus. The bonus amount is dependent upon how well the company does as a whole and how well you perform your duties according to a set of predetermined Key Performance Indicators. The company's primary business is selling medical malpractice insurance to physicians. It collects premiums and invests those premiums in a variety of investments as determined by the California Department of Insurance. In the past, the company has developed a hotel property in the City of Yountville. Currently, your employer has an application with Napa County for a 300 bed hotel and business conference center to be located adjacent to its headquarters building on the corner of Highways 12 and 29. The proposed project of the company is approximately six miles south of any proposed development of a similar nature that might occur at the 25th Agricultural District Association
